title: Quantum simulations of ultrafast optical spectroscopy of semiconductors on digital quantum computers in the semi-classical approximation
authors: Mykhailo Klymenko, Bahar Goldozian, Thong Hoang, Jared H. Cole, Muhammad Usman
abstract: We present a digital quantum simulation framework for ultrafast optical spectroscopy of semiconductor materials. The framework is based on Brillouin-zone discretization and the second-quantization formalism, and is designed as a quantum alternative to classical simulations based on the semiconductor Bloch equations. Its current capabilities include quantum simulations of linear absorption and optical gain spectra, incorporating Lorentzian broadening, finite-temperature band-filling effects, and reduced-dimensionality effects. Benchmark comparisons with classical simulations for GaAs demonstrate quantitative agreement in the noiseless limit. The inclusion of realistic hardware noise of NISQ-era quantum computers effectively manifests itself as an additional source of scattering processes, resulting in increased spectral broadening. While no exponential quantum advantage is expected in the single-particle approximation, the framework naturally extends to many-body regimes where classical simulations face the hierarchy problem and exponential scaling and provable quantum advantage will be possible. The quantum simulations considered in this work capture central elements of semiconductor spectroscopy, the aspects such as open quantum systems, light-matter interactions, statistical mechanics, non-equilibrium quantum dynamics, and many-body physics. As such, it provides a physically motivated and scalable model for benchmarking quantum computers in applications to complex, real-world problems.
summary: This work establishes a quantum simulation framework to model how semiconductors interact with ultrafast light pulses. By mapping the complex fermionic dynamics onto qubits, it allows for benchmarking quantum computers against established classical methods. The results demonstrate the framework's capability while critically analyzing the impact of realistic hardware noise on spectroscopic measurements.
main_problem: Developing a digital quantum simulation framework to model ultrafast optical spectroscopy in semiconductor materials.
main_result: The framework successfully simulates key spectroscopic features (absorption/gain) and shows quantitative agreement with classical methods in the noiseless limit, while also characterizing the impact of realistic NISQ noise.
method: The simulation maps the fermionic Hamiltonian onto qubits using the Jordan-Wigner transformation and evolves the system using the Suzuki-Trotter formula for time evolution.
model_or_system: The system is semiconductor materials (e.g., GaAs) modeled by a Hamiltonian including kinetic, Coulomb, and time-dependent light-matter interaction terms. The dynamics are treated as an open quantum system.
key_observables: Induced dipole moment/polarization, electric susceptibility $\chi(\omega)$, and the linear absorption/optical gain spectra $\alpha(\omega)$.
important_parameters: Ultrafast time scales, finite temperatures ($T=3$ K to $T=30$ K), and the presence of realistic hardware noise.
main_assumptions: The semiclassical approximation is used (classical field, quantum matter), and the simulation often relies on the quasi-equilibrium approximation for temperature effects.
figures_summary: Figures compare microscopic polarization and linear absorption spectra across classical, noiseless quantum, and noisy quantum simulations for GaAs, illustrating the effect of noise and temperature.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the physical problem and Hamiltonian, detailing the theoretical mapping to qubits (JWT), outlining the time evolution via Trotterization, and finally presenting comparative results across different noise and temperature regimes.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, physically motivated application for quantum computation—simulating complex, non-equilibrium quantum dynamics—and directly addresses the challenges of noise in near-term quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Mapping spectroscopic experiments onto qubit Hamiltonian simulations for (a) a two-level system and (b) a direct-band two-band semiconductor. In the semiclassical ap- proximation, light–matter interactions are modeled through time-dependent induced dipole moments and implemented on a quantum computer using parameterized entangling two- qubit gates.
FIG. 2. Quantum circuit diagram for one Trotter step of the Hamiltonian simulation

FIG. 3. Example quantum circuit for semiconductor time- domain spectroscopy simulations for N = 3 time steps. The gray ribbon denotes the quantum register of size 2K, where K is the number of wave vectors in the discretized Brillouin zone, and the factor of two corresponds to the number of energy bands.

FIG. 5. Comparison of classical (black line) and quantum (blue line) simulations of the time evolution of the microscopic polarization for two wave vectors: (a) k = 0 nm−1 and (b) k = 5 nm−1 in a noiseless environment using 104 shots. The red trace indicates the temporal profile of the probe pulse.

FIG. 4. Real (upper row) and imaginary (bottom row) parts of the microscopic polarization in semiconductor medium in- duced by an ultra-short Gaussian optical pulse. The results are presented for a) classical simulations, b) noiseless quan- tum simulations, and c) quantum simulations with realistic noise models.
